2018 · EMNLP · Benchmarks & Evals

Spider: A Large-Scale Human-Labeled Dataset for Complex and Cross-Domain Semantic Parsing and Text-to-SQL Task

10,181 questions paired with 5,693 hand-written SQL queries across 200 multi-table databases, split so every test-time database is unseen in training — the first text-to-SQL benchmark built to test schema generalization instead of query memorization.

♪ listen — audio episode
3-voice deep dive · mp3 ↗ · rss feed ↗
Tao Yu, Rui Zhang, Kai Yang, Michihiro Yasunaga, Dongxu Wang, Zifan Li, James Ma, Irene Li, Qingning Yao, Shanelle Roman, Zilin Zhang, Dragomir R. Radev
venue EMNLP 2018 citations 1,960 topic Benchmarks & Evals source ↗ listen ♪
// citation count as of 2026-07
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

By 2018, Seq2Seq-based semantic parsers were reporting over 80% exact-match accuracy on complex benchmarks like ATIS and GeoQuery — numbers that looked like the field was nearly solved. It wasn't. Those datasets are single-database: the same handful of SQL programs recur in both train and test, expanded only by paraphrasing the natural-language question 4–10 ways per program. A model can hit high accuracy by memorizing which SQL template a question-shape maps to, never learning to parse a genuinely new query. Finegan-Dollak et al. (2018) demonstrated exactly this: switch from a question-based split to a program-based split (no identical SQL program shared across train/test) and every state-of-the-art system's accuracy collapses.

WikiSQL (Zhong et al., 2017) fixed half the problem — its 80,654 questions span 24,241 different single-table databases, split so that test-time tables are unseen, forcing genuine schema generalization. But it bought that scale by simplifying the SQL: a single SELECT column plus an aggregation and a WHERE clause, one table at a time. No JOIN, no GROUP BY/HAVING, no nested subqueries, no multi-table foreign-key reasoning — exactly the constructs that make real-world SQL hard.

No dataset combined both axes: multi-table schema complexity and cross-domain generalization to unseen databases at test time. Spider was built to require both at once, and to do it with SQL complex enough that a model can no longer sidestep genuine parsing by pattern-matching a small template inventory.

// how it works

The method

Spider's 200 databases (138 domains, multi-table, foreign-keyed) come from three sources: about 70 hand-collected from college database courses, SQL tutorials, and textbook examples; about 40 pulled from DatabaseAnswers's schema library (over 1,000 schema-only data models) and converted to populated SQLite databases with an online population tool, then manually corrected; and about 90 built by selecting related tables out of WikiSQL and stitching them into genuine relational schemas with foreign keys (adding intersection tables where needed to link them). Column and table names that used database-style abbreviations (e.g. "stu_id") were manually normalized back to full words so the task stays a semantic-parsing problem, not an abbreviation-guessing one.

Eleven Yale CS students spent roughly 1,000 person-hours across a five-stage annotation pipeline (Figure 2): Database Collection & Creation (150h) → Question and SQL Annotation (500h, 20–50 examples per database) → SQL Review (150h) → Question Review & Paraphrase (150h) → Final Review & Processing (150h). Three protocol rules governed annotation: SQL pattern coverage (every database's queries must collectively exercise SELECT with aggregation, WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING, ORDER BY, LIMIT, JOIN, INTERSECT, EXCEPT, UNION, NOT IN, OR, AND, EXISTS, LIKE, and nested subqueries, with every table touched at least once); SQL consistency (annotators converge on one canonical SQL shape when a question admits multiple equivalent queries); and question clarity (no ambiguous questions, none requiring outside common-sense knowledge). The result — illustrated by Figure 1's own example, a JOIN across instructor/department with a GROUP BY and a nested subquery — is 10,181 questions and 5,693 unique complex SQL queries, roughly 1.8 paraphrased questions per query on average.

The paper's central design choice is the database split, defined against a new cross-domain text-to-SQL task: since Spider's databases carry foreign keys and 200 of them exist, the corpus can be partitioned by database rather than by example. Combined with 752 additional queries pulled in from six prior single-domain datasets (Restaurants, GeoQuery, Scholar, Academic, IMDB, Yelp), the 206 total databases split into 146 train / 20 dev / 40 test, with every question tied to a given database assigned to exactly one split — so a database (and every one of its SQL patterns) seen at test time was never seen during training. This is contrasted throughout the paper with an example split (8,659 train / 1,034 dev / 2,147 test, examples randomly assigned so the same database can appear in both train and test) — the setting most prior text-to-SQL work implicitly used.

To make performance analyzable, queries are bucketed into four hardness tiers — easy, medium, hard, extra hard (Figure 3) — by counting SQL components, selections, and conditions: a query is "hard" once it has more than two SELECT columns, more than two WHERE conditions, and a two-column GROUP BY, or contains EXCEPT or a nested subquery; "extra hard" stacks further on top of that. Against the combined total of eight prior text-to-SQL datasets, Spider contains roughly 2× more nested queries and roughly 10× more ORDER BY/LIMIT and GROUP BY/HAVING components (1,335 ORDER BY, 1,491 GROUP BY, 844 nested, and 388 HAVING instances in Spider alone). The average database has 27.6 columns and 8.8 foreign keys; the average question is about 13 tokens and its gold SQL about 21 tokens.

Three evaluation metrics are defined. Component Matching decomposes a predicted and gold query into per-clause sets (SELECT, WHERE, GROUP BY, ORDER BY, KEYWORDS) and scores set-overlap F1 per component, so ordering within a clause doesn't matter. Exact Matching requires every component to match exactly — the query is correct only if all clause-level sets agree. Execution Accuracy is proposed as a complementary check (does the query actually return the right rows) but the paper is explicit that it is not implemented in this release: value prediction is out of scope for the initial task (a list of gold values is handed to the model rather than generated), so execution can't yet be run end-to-end.

F1component = 2 · P · RP + R // P, R computed by set-overlap between the predicted and gold decomposition of one SQL clause (e.g. SELECT's {agg, column} pairs) — order-insensitive by construction
// what they found

The results

Five baseline semantic parsers were adapted to the task: Seq2Seq, Seq2Seq+Attention, Seq2Seq+Copying (general sequence decoders), and the sketch/slot-filling models SQLNet and TypeSQL (originally built for WikiSQL, extended here with ORDER BY and GROUP BY modules). The headline number from the abstract — 12.4% exact-match accuracy on the test set — belongs to SQLNet under the database split, the best score any model achieves in that setting (Table 2 below). TypeSQL, which tops the easier example split at 33.0% test / 34.4% dev by leveraging column-name and content-derived type information, collapses to 8.2% test / 8.0% dev under the database split — the largest relative drop of any model, showing it had learned schema-specific cues rather than transferable parsing.

Method (database split, test)EasyMediumHardExtra HardAll
Seq2Seq11.91.91.30.53.7
Seq2Seq+Attention14.92.52.01.14.8
Seq2Seq+Copying15.43.42.01.15.3
TypeSQL19.67.63.80.88.2
SQLNet (best)26.212.66.61.312.4

At the component level (Table 3 in the paper), every model struggles hardest on WHERE-clause prediction — SQLNet's best F1 there is only 19.8 under the database split, versus 64.0 on KEYWORDS — because WHERE clauses typically involve multiple columns and operators drawn from a large candidate space. Figure 4 (not reproduced here) plots accuracy against a database's number of foreign keys and shows a clean monotonic decline: roughly 10–11% exact-match at 1–4 foreign keys falling to about 1% at more than 10, since more foreign keys mean more candidate join paths through an increasingly tangled schema. The plain Seq2Seq variants mostly fail to emit valid SQL at all; the sketch-based models produce syntactically valid queries far more often but are structurally incapable of generating nested queries or EXCEPT/INTERSECT, since their decoding is constrained to a fixed set of SQL sketch templates.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

Exact Matching decomposes a query into per-clause sets rather than doing a raw string comparison, which sidesteps trivial reordering issues (e.g. two SELECT columns listed in a different order). But it is still a syntactic proxy: it cannot recognize a query that is semantically equivalent to the gold SQL but structurally different — a different-but-valid JOIN order, a subquery rewritten as a JOIN, or a condition moved from HAVING into an equivalent nested WHERE. The paper's own "SQL consistency" annotation rule — forcing annotators to converge on one canonical SQL shape whenever multiple queries would produce the same answer — is an implicit admission of this gap: it patches the label at annotation time rather than fixing the metric, so a model that arrives at a correct but differently-shaped query is simply marked wrong.

Execution Accuracy is introduced as the natural complement (does the query actually return the right rows, regardless of shape), and the paper is admirably candid that it also has a failure mode: a wrong query can accidentally return the same result as the gold query (e.g. both evaluate to NULL or an empty set), producing a false positive. The catch is that in this release execution accuracy is only proposed, not implemented — the task excludes value prediction and hands models a gold value list rather than generating it, so end-to-end execution wasn't runnable yet. "We can use both to complement each other" is the right instinct, but only one of the two metrics was actually available to authors at release.

Contamination is the elephant in the room for any 2018 benchmark still in active use: Spider has been public for over seven years, is bundled into essentially every text-to-SQL toolkit and leaderboard, and its train split — full schemas, questions, and gold SQL — is mirrored across GitHub and Hugging Face. A modern LLM's cross-domain "generalization" score on well-known Spider databases (e.g. concert_singer, car_1) cannot be cleanly separated from having memorized these widely-circulated question–schema–SQL triples during pretraining. That is precisely why Spider now functions mostly as a saturated baseline — later work reports fine-tuned exact-match scores well above 85% — motivating a chain of successors (Spider-Realistic, Spider-SYN, Dr.Spider, BIRD, Spider 2.0) rather than treating this release as the finish line. Separately, the databases themselves skew toy-scale (mean 27.6 columns per DB) and roughly two-thirds were synthetically populated or converted from schema-only sources rather than reflecting messy, authentic production data — a gap BIRD (2023) explicitly targeted by moving to 95 real, GB-scale databases.

baselines

Seq2Seq / +Attention / +Copying, SQLNet, and TypeSQL were reasonable state of the art for late 2018, but none were purpose-built to encode multi-table schemas with foreign keys — that architecture (relation-aware schema encoding) arrived a year later with RAT-SQL, so the reported 12.4% ceiling reflects genuine task difficulty more than a fair "best possible 2018 model" bound.

contamination

Public for 7+ years, mirrored everywhere, and folded into pretraining-scale web corpora — LLM-era scores on Spider's well-known databases increasingly measure memorization of published question-schema-SQL triples alongside genuine parsing.

what's missed

Exact/Component Matching is a structural-equivalence proxy: it silently penalizes a correct query phrased with a different (but valid) join order, subquery-vs-JOIN rewrite, or clause restructuring. Execution Accuracy, the proposed fix, is explicitly not computed in this release.

would raise confidence

Ship execution accuracy alongside exact match from day one; add a semantic-equivalence checker instead of relying on annotators converging on one canonical shape; run periodic contamination audits given the dataset's age; and prefer held-out/rotating splits — the direction Spider 2.0 and Dr.Spider later took.

// bottom line

Spider's database split was the right fix for the field's biggest problem in 2018 — memorized single-database templates — and its 12.4% opening exact-match score proved the cross-domain task was genuinely hard. But exact/component matching is a syntactic proxy that can't tell a correct-but-differently-shaped query from a wrong one, execution accuracy shipped as a stated intention rather than a working metric, and seven-plus years of public exposure make Spider's numbers for modern LLMs an increasingly murky mix of parsing skill and memorization — exactly why the field kept building successors instead of stopping here.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. Spider's headline contribution is the database split — partitioning by schema rather than by example, so a test-time database is guaranteed unseen in training — forcing models to generalize across domains, not just across paraphrases of the same query.
  2. It beats WikiSQL's scale-without-complexity and ATIS/GeoQuery's complexity-without-scale simultaneously: 10,181 questions and 5,693 SQL queries across 200 multi-table databases spanning 138 domains, with real JOINs, nesting, and set operations WikiSQL's single-table SQL never required.
  3. The 12.4% exact-match ceiling for the best 2018 baseline (SQLNet) on the database split — versus that same family's much higher scores on the easier example split — is itself the paper's real finding: schema generalization, not raw query complexity, is what broke every model tested.
